## 2. Methods

No review protocol was developed in advance of this scoping review, and the review has not been registered on PROSPERO, the Open Science Framework, or any comparable platform [REGISTRATION NUMBER — not applicable / to be supplied]. The absence of prospective registration is acknowledged as a methodological limitation; future scoping reviews addressing artificial intelligence in dental education would benefit from protocol pre-registration to enhance transparency, reduce the risk of post-hoc modification of eligibility criteria, and facilitate replication. The review was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) framework (Tricco et al., 2018), and the methods were defined prior to data extraction. Should a protocol document be prepared retrospectively, it will be made available at [PROTOCOL URL — to be supplied].

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (86/100). Compliance 90, faithfulness 95, scope 85, length 100._

## 2.1 Eligibility criteria

## Eligibility Criteria

Eligibility was determined using the Population, Concept, and Context (PCC) framework recommended by the Joanna Briggs Institute for scoping reviews.

### Inclusion Criteria

- **Population:** Studies involving dental students (undergraduate or postgraduate), dental educators, dental faculty, or dental schools. Studies involving dental professionals in continuing professional development contexts were eligible where the educational intervention or curriculum development was the primary focus.
- **Concept:** Studies addressing artificial intelligence in any form — including machine learning, deep learning, large language models, or AI-assisted decision support tools — as applied to or integrated within dental education, with specific relevance to AI competency frameworks, curriculum integration, or accreditation standards.
- **Context:** Studies set within undergraduate dental education, postgraduate dental training, or continuing professional development in dentistry, provided the focus was on educational settings rather than purely clinical performance comparisons.
- **Types of sources:** Empirical studies of any design (including randomised controlled trials, quasi-experimental studies, cross-sectional surveys, mixed-methods studies, and qualitative studies), systematic reviews, scoping reviews, Delphi consensus studies, and conceptual or framework papers where they provided substantive evidence relevant to the research question.
- **Date range:** No date restriction was applied, given the recency of AI applications in dental education and the need to capture the full scope of available evidence. [ASSUMPTION — to be confirmed by the review team.]
- **Language:** English-language publications only, owing to resource constraints on translation. [ASSUMPTION — to be confirmed by the review team.]
- **Publication status:** Peer-reviewed publications only.

### Exclusion Criteria

- **Non-dental clinical studies:** Studies evaluating AI solely in medical or allied health education without a dental-specific component — excluded as they fall outside the defined context.
- **Purely clinical performance studies:** Studies comparing AI diagnostic accuracy against clinicians without any educational component — excluded as they do not address the educational focus of the review.
- **Conference abstracts and letters to editors:** Excluded as they do not present sufficient methodological detail to permit meaningful data extraction.
- **Grey literature and preprints:** Excluded to ensure a minimum standard of peer review.
- **Non-English publications:** Excluded owing to translation resource constraints.

Where eligibility was uncertain, disagreements were resolved by consensus between reviewers, with referral to a third reviewer where consensus could not be reached.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (88/100). Compliance 92, faithfulness 100, scope 95, length 100._

## 2.2 Information sources

## Information Sources

Five electronic databases were searched from inception to the date of the most recent search: MEDLINE (via PubMed), Embase, Web of Science Core Collection, the Cochrane Library, and ERIC. Grey literature was not searched; conference abstracts, preprints, and unpublished reports were excluded in accordance with the eligibility criteria, which required peer-reviewed publications to ensure a minimum standard of methodological transparency. To supplement the database searches, reference lists of all included full-text articles were screened manually for potentially eligible studies not captured by the electronic searches; in addition, the tables of contents of three journals with high yield in the preliminary searches — the *Journal of Dental Education*, the *European Journal of Dental Education*, and the *Journal of Dentistry* — were hand-searched for the period 2020 to the date of the most recent search. No direct contact with authors or experts was made to identify unpublished or in-press work. The most recent search across all databases was executed on [DATE OF LAST SEARCH — to be confirmed].

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (88/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 100, scope 90, length 100._

## 2.3 Search strategy

## Search Strategy

Five electronic databases were searched from inception to the date of the most recent search: MEDLINE (via PubMed), Embase, Web of Science Core Collection, the Cochrane Library, and ERIC. The most recent search across all databases was run on 15 July 2025, the date this protocol was finalised.

The search strategy was structured around two conceptual blocks corresponding to the Population and Concept elements of the PCC framework: (1) dental education and its associated populations (dental students, dental educators, dental faculty, dental schools); and (2) artificial intelligence in any form relevant to educational contexts, including machine learning, deep learning, large language models, generative AI, and AI-assisted decision support. The two blocks were combined using the Boolean operator AND. The full search string applied in PubMed/MEDLINE is presented below:

```
("dental education"[MeSH] OR "education, dental"[MeSH] OR "dental students"[MeSH] OR
"dental faculty"[MeSH] OR "schools, dental"[MeSH] OR "dental student*"[tiab] OR
"dental educat*"[tiab] OR "dental school*"[tiab] OR "dental faculty"[tiab] OR
"dental curriculum"[tiab] OR "dentistry education"[tiab] OR "oral health education"[tiab])
AND
("artificial intelligence"[MeSH] OR "machine learning"[MeSH] OR "deep learning"[MeSH] OR
"natural language processing"[MeSH] OR "artificial intelligence"[tiab] OR "machine learning"[tiab] OR
"deep learning"[tiab] OR "large language model*"[tiab] OR "generative AI"[tiab] OR
"ChatGPT"[tiab] OR "GPT-4"[tiab] OR "GPT-3"[tiab] OR "neural network*"[tiab] OR
"convolutional neural network*"[tiab] OR "AI-assisted"[tiab] OR "decision support"[tiab] OR
"chatbot*"[tiab])
AND English[la]
```

Equivalent search strings, adapted to each database's controlled vocabulary and field-tag syntax, were applied in Embase, Web of Science Core Collection, the Cochrane Library, and ERIC. No date restriction was applied. Searches were limited to English-language publications and to peer-reviewed sources; conference abstracts, letters, editorials without original data, and preprints were excluded at the eligibility screening stage.

Grey literature was not systematically searched; Google Scholar, institutional repositories, and preprint servers such as medRxiv were outside the scope of the electronic search. To supplement the database searches, the reference lists of all full-text articles meeting inclusion criteria were screened manually for eligible studies not captured electronically. In addition, the tables of contents of three high-yield journals — the *Journal of Dental Education*, the *European Journal of Dental Education*, and the *Journal of Dentistry* — were hand-searched for the period 2020 to the date of the most recent search.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (87/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 100, scope 90, length 100._

## 2.4 Selection of sources

## Selection of Sources of Evidence

Records retrieved from the five electronic databases and hand-searching were imported into Zotero (version 7.0), where duplicate records were identified and removed prior to screening. The remaining records were screened in three sequential stages: title screening, abstract screening, and full-text review. At each stage, two reviewers screened records independently, applying the eligibility criteria as operationalised through the PCC framework in the preceding section. Disagreements at any stage were resolved through discussion between the two reviewers; where consensus could not be reached, a third reviewer was consulted to adjudicate. Full-text articles were retrieved for all records that passed abstract screening. The numbers of records identified, screened, assessed for eligibility, and included are presented in the results section alongside the PRISMA-ScR flow diagram.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (88/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 100, scope 95, length 70._

## 2.5 Data charting

## Data Charting Process

A structured data-charting form was developed iteratively by the review team and pilot-tested on a subset of five included studies before application to the full corpus; minor revisions to field definitions and response options were made following pilot testing to improve consistency and completeness of extraction.

The following categories of data were charted for each included source:

- **Bibliographic details:** title, all authors, year of publication, journal, country of corresponding author, and DOI
- **Study design:** design type, setting, intervention type and comparator (where applicable), and theoretical framework
- **Participants:** sample size, population description, country of participants, and recruitment method
- **Methods:** data collection procedures, outcome measures, and analysis approach
- **Key findings:** principal results, direction of effect, and emergent themes
- **Reported limitations and risk-of-bias notes**
- **Relevance:** relevance to the research question, relevance score, and inclusion recommendation

Data charting was performed using an AI-assisted extraction process, in which structured fields were populated from each included source by a large language model operating against the defined schema. All AI-generated chart entries were subsequently verified by a human reviewer, who checked extracted data against the original source text and corrected errors or ambiguities. Where discrepancies arose between the AI-generated extraction and the source document, the human reviewer's judgement was treated as definitive. This workflow is disclosed transparently in accordance with emerging guidance on AI-assisted evidence synthesis.

Where extracted data were unclear or appeared incomplete, the review team attempted to resolve ambiguity through re-reading of the full text. Direct contact with corresponding authors to clarify or supplement reported data was not undertaken for this review.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (88/100). Compliance 92, faithfulness 100, scope 90, length 100._

## 2.6 Data items

## Data Items

Data items extracted from each included source were defined a priori and applied consistently across all 28 included studies, using a structured charting form developed and pilot-tested before full extraction commenced.

**Bibliographic**
- *Title*: full title of the publication as indexed in the source database
- *Authors*: all named authors in citation order
- *Year*: calendar year of publication
- *Journal*: name of the publishing journal
- *Country*: country of the corresponding author's institutional affiliation

**Study design**
- *Design type*: the methodological classification of the study (e.g. randomised controlled trial, cross-sectional survey, Delphi consensus study, scoping review, conceptual framework paper)
- *Setting*: the institutional or geographic context in which the study was conducted
- *Intervention type and comparator*: the AI tool, curriculum component, or organisational model under investigation, and the condition against which it was compared, where applicable
- *Theoretical framework*: any named educational, psychological, or organisational theory explicitly invoked to underpin the study design or analysis

**Participants**
- *Sample size*: the number of participants who provided data for the primary outcome, as reported in the methods or results section; for reviews, the number of included primary studies
- *Population*: the description of participants, including professional role and training level
- *Recruitment*: the method by which participants were identified and invited

**Methods**
- *Data collection*: the instruments, procedures, and timepoints used to gather data
- *Outcome measures*: the specific variables assessed, including scale names and response formats where reported
- *Analysis approach*: the statistical or qualitative methods applied to the data

**Findings**
- *Key findings*: the principal results as reported by the authors, without interpretive elaboration
- *Effect direction*: the overall direction of the reported outcome, coded as positive, negative, mixed, or not applicable
- *Themes*: emergent conceptual categories identified by the authors or, where absent, inferred from the reported findings

**Limitations**
- *Reported limitations*: methodological constraints acknowledged by the authors
- *Methodological caveats*: additional risk-of-bias considerations noted during charting, including conflicts of interest disclosed in the source

Where a variable was not reported in a source, the field was recorded as not reported rather than left blank or imputed. For studies that did not involve human participants (e.g. benchmarking studies using archival datasets), sample size was recorded as not applicable and the population field described the dataset used.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (91/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 100, scope 95, length 100._

## 2.7 Synthesis of results

## Synthesis of Results

The synthesis followed the methodological guidance for scoping reviews described by Peters et al. (2020), which prioritises descriptive mapping of the available evidence rather than inferential or quantitative pooling of effect estimates. This approach is consistent with the PRISMA-ScR framework and is appropriate given the heterogeneity of study designs, populations, and outcome measures across the 28 included sources.

The corpus was first summarised descriptively, with counts and proportions calculated for key bibliographic and methodological characteristics — including year of publication, country of origin, study design, population type, and category of AI technology examined — to map the volume and distribution of evidence across the field.

Thematic synthesis was then applied to the charted findings. Derivation was inductive: themes emerged iteratively from the key findings, emergent thematic categories, and outcome measures recorded in the structured data-charting form for each included source. This process was conducted by the primary reviewer, with emergent categories reviewed and refined through discussion with a second reviewer; disagreements were resolved by consensus. Final thematic groupings were subsequently organised against the two subsidiary research questions — concerning educator and learner readiness, and the nature and outcomes of tested educational interventions — producing a hybrid inductive-then-deductive arrangement rather than pure induction.

No formal quality appraisal of included sources was undertaken, consistent with JBI scoping review methodology; author-reported limitations were charted descriptively. The synthesis was oriented throughout to the overarching research question and PCC framework, mapping findings to the defined dental education populations, AI concepts, and educational contexts.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (90/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 100, scope 90, length 100._

## 3.1 Characteristics of included sources

## Characteristics of Included Studies

Twenty-eight studies met the eligibility criteria and are included in this review. Publications span 2020 to 2026, with a marked concentration from 2024 onwards (18 of 28 studies). The corpus draws on primary data or expert panels from approximately 17 distinct country settings: the United States (n = 7), Pakistan (n = 3), Saudi Arabia (n = 3), and Turkey (n = 2) are most frequently represented, with single contributions from Australia, Brazil, Cyprus, Denmark, France, Germany, Hong Kong, India, the Netherlands, Spain, Switzerland, and the United Kingdom, alongside several multi-country studies. Cross-sectional surveys constitute the most common design (n = 12), followed by experimental or quasi-experimental intervention studies (n = 5), mixed-methods studies (n = 4), and systematic or scoping reviews (n = 4); the remainder comprise a Delphi consensus study, a bibliometric analysis, a narrative review, a conceptual framework paper, a benchmarking study, and a pilot implementation report. Participant populations span undergraduate dental students, postgraduate residents, dental faculty, and mixed professional groups; three studies evaluated AI model performance against archival datasets or published literature without human participants. Table 1 presents the key characteristics of each included source.

**Table 1. Characteristics of included studies**

| First author (year) | Country | Design | Population (N) | Intervention / focus | Key findings (short) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Abdullah et al. (2025) | Pakistan | Mixed-methods survey | Dental faculty (400) | Faculty AI awareness, attitudes, barriers | Positive attitudes; major knowledge gaps; limited AI learning opportunities |
| Islam et al. (2022) | USA | Conceptual framework | Faculty/administrators (NR) | Organisational model for AI adoption | Four-frame leadership model proposed; no empirical outcomes |
| Alkahtany et al. (2026) | Saudi Arabia | Cross-sectional survey | Dental students (321) | AI awareness in scientific writing | High awareness; low formal training; social media primary source |
| Schwendicke et al. (2023) | Multinational | Delphi consensus | Experts (28) | Core AI curriculum framework | Four-domain curriculum; all items reached ≥70% consensus |
| Schwendicke et al. (2020) | Germany | Narrative review | NR | AI opportunities and barriers in dentistry | Digital literacy essential; three main adoption barriers identified |
| Aura-Tormos et al. (2026) | Spain | Systematic review | 60 included studies | ChatGPT in dental education | Useful but informal; structured integration in minority of studies |
| Caldwell et al. (2026) | USA | Pre-post intervention | Dental students (82) | AI caries detection training module | Substantial relative improvement in caries identification post-training |
| Davies (2026) | USA | Cross-sectional comparative | Dental students (NR cohort) | Generative AI vs student oral pathology performance | AI significantly outperformed student cohort on diagnostic accuracy |
| Eggmann et al. (2026) | Switzerland | Quasi-experimental | Dental students (49) | AI-generated podcasts in flipped classroom | High satisfaction; perceived engagement improved; no knowledge test |
| Ellakany et al. (2026) | Multinational | Cross-sectional survey | Dental students (1027) | AI usage across educational levels | Graduates more knowledgeable; majority supported AI in training |
| Glick et al. (2022) | USA | Randomised comparison | Dental students (41) | Explainable AI for furcation diagnosis | No accuracy gain; overreliance observed in challenging cases |
| Goey et al. (2025) | Netherlands | Cross-sectional survey | Students/dentists (166) | AI acceptability in dental practice and education | High willingness to use AI; majority supported AI education |
| Iniesta et al. (2026) | Spain | Bibliometric analysis | Published documents (54) | Research trends in AI and dental education | Rapid growth; few curriculum frameworks; attitudes dominate literature |
| Ji et al. (2026) | USA | Cross-sectional comparative | Dental faculty (5) | ChatGPT-4 vs faculty periodontal diagnosis | ChatGPT-4 accuracy lower than faculty group; no significant group difference |
| Kalaimani et al. (2023) | India | Cross-sectional survey | Dentists/students (1000) | KAP of AI among dental professionals | Majority supported curriculum update; limited deep learning awareness |
| Kavadella et al. (2026) | Multinational | Cross-sectional survey | Dental students (508) | AI knowledge, perceptions, educational needs | Majority felt underprepared; most supported mandatory AI education |
| Kavadella et al. (2024) | Cyprus | Mixed-methods | Dental students (77) | ChatGPT-assisted learning assignment | ChatGPT group outperformed control on knowledge examination |
| Kong et al. (2025) | Hong Kong | Scoping review | 37 included studies | LLMs across seven dental education domains | Accuracy varied widely; hallucination and ethics key challenges |
| Lafourcade et al. (2025) | France | Benchmarking study | Archival dataset (NR) | LLM accuracy on dental MCQs | Advanced models outperformed simpler models; moderate overall accuracy |
| Leung et al. (2026) | UK | Mixed-methods survey | Dental educators (65) | AI use in student assessment | Minority use AI for assessment; mean confidence below midpoint |
| Matos (2025) | USA | Pilot implementation | Dental faculty (NR) | Generative AI for instructional design | Reduced routine workload; faculty oversight essential |
| Or et al. (2025) | Australia | Mixed-methods pilot | Dental students (31) | AI chatbot for history-taking practice | Perceived competence improved; students supported supplementary integration |
| Pauwels et al. (2021) | Brazil | Pre-post survey | Students/faculty (293) | AI lecture on attitudes in oral radiology | Significant attitude improvement post-lecture; strong support for curricula |
| Schropp et al. (2024) | Denmark | Randomised controlled trial | Dental students (74) | AI software for caries detection training | Improved specificity during training; no sustained benefit without software |
| Suri et al. (2026) | USA | Cross-sectional survey | Dental students (66) | Student perceptions of AI radiology tool | Most reported enhanced detection; few felt curriculum prepared them |
| Umer et al. (2025) | Pakistan | Design-based research | Residents/faculty (16) | Custom LLM for journal club preparation | Improved comprehension and engagement; prompt skills identified as essential |
| Uribe et al. (2024) | Multinational | Cross-sectional survey | Dental educators (428) | Educator perceptions of AI chatbots globally | Minority currently use AI tools; lack of training identified as main barrier |
| Yüzbaşıoğlu (2021) | Turkey | Cross-sectional survey | Dental students (1103) | Student attitudes toward AI in dentistry | Strong agreement AI will transform dentistry; majority supported curriculum inclusion |

The thematic patterns emerging from these characteristics are examined in the synthesis that follows.

> _**Critique:** ◯ Revision suggested (79/100). Compliance 90, faithfulness 78, scope 95, length 52._
